arr = (i, i = 1, 4)     ! Incorrect usage

program expr2
    implicit none
    character(len=8) :: s
    s = "lfortran"
    print*, "s:", s(-1:4)

s:

```Fortran
MODULE input_module
    implicit none
    INTERFACE test_interface
       MODULE PROCEDURE test_01, test_01, test_01
    END INTERFACE test_interface
 CONTAINS
 
    SUBROUTINE test_01 (x)
        implicit none
        integer , intent(in):: x 
    END SUBROUTINE test_01

 END MODULE input_module

```
This should raise an error as there are 3 modules with the same name.
there's no harm in duplicating it, but it's not a good practice.

compiling the following program with LFortran, instead of getting a semantic error, the compiler produces a code generation error: 

  ```
  program main
      integer :: x = 'Apple'
  end program
```

Output : 
 ```lfortran test.f90
; ModuleID = 'LFortran'
source_filename = "LFortran"

@main.x = internal global i32 0
@0 = private unnamed_addr constant [6 x i8] c"Apple\00", align 1
@1 = private unnamed_addr constant [6 x i8] c"Apple\00", align 1

define i32 @main(i32 %0, i8** %1) {
.entry:
  store i8* getelementptr inbounds ([6 x i8], [6 x i8]* @0, i32 0, i32 0), i32* @main.x, align 8
  call void @_lpython_call_initial_functions(i32 %0, i8** %1)
  store i8* getelementptr inbounds ([6 x i8], [6 x i8]* @1, i32 0, i32 0), i32* @main.x, align 8
  br label %return

return:                                           ; preds = %.entry
  ret i32 0
}

declare void @_lpython_call_initial_functions(i32, i8**)
asr_to_llvm: module failed verification. Error:
Stored value type does not match pointer operand type!
  store i8* getelementptr inbounds ([6 x i8], [6 x i8]* @0, i32 0, i32 0), i32* @main.x, align 8
 i32Stored value type does not match pointer operand type!
  store i8* getelementptr inbounds ([6 x i8], [6 x i8]* @1, i32 0, i32 0), i32* @main.x, align 8
 i32
code generation error: asr_to_llvm: module failed verification. Error:
Stored value type does not match pointer operand type!
  store i8* getelementptr inbounds ([6 x i8], [6 x i8]* @0, i32 0, i32 0), i32* @main.x, align 8
 i32Stored value type does not match pointer operand type!
  store i8* getelementptr inbounds ([6 x i8], [6 x i8]* @1, i32 0, i32 0), i32* @main.x, align 8
 i32


Note: Please report unclear, confusing or incorrect messages as bugs at
https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/issues.
```
When I compile the same program using gfortran, I get the expected semantic error:
```
gfortran test.f90
test.f90:2:18:

    2 |     integer :: x = 'Apple'
      |                  1
Error: Cannot convert CHARACTER(5) to INTEGER(4) at (1)
```
LFortran should throw a ``semantic error`` instead of proceeding to code generation and ultimately resulting in a ``code generation error.``  The error message should indicate that the string 'Apple' cannot be assigned to an integer. 
I would also like to help resolve this issue.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/4776/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/4776/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

compiling the following program with LFortran, instead of getting a semantic error, the compiler produces a code generation error: 

  ``` f90
  program main
      integer :: x = 'Apple'
  end program
```

Output : 
 ``` llvm 
lfortran test.f90
; ModuleID = 'LFortran'
source_filename = "LFortran"

@main.x = internal global i32 0
@0 = private unnamed_addr constant [6 x i8] c"Apple\00", align 1
@1 = private unnamed_addr constant [6 x i8] c"Apple\00", align 1

define i32 @main(i32 %0, i8** %1) {
.entry:
  store i8* getelementptr inbounds ([6 x i8], [6 x i8]* @0, i32 0, i32 0), i32* @main.x, align 8
  call void @_lpython_call_initial_functions(i32 %0, i8** %1)
  store i8* getelementptr inbounds ([6 x i8], [6 x i8]* @1, i32 0, i32 0), i32* @main.x, align 8
  br label %return

return:                                           ; preds = %.entry
  ret i32 0
}

declare void @_lpython_call_initial_functions(i32, i8**)
asr_to_llvm: module failed verification. Error:
Stored value type does not match pointer operand type!
  store i8* getelementptr inbounds ([6 x i8], [6 x i8]* @0, i32 0, i32 0), i32* @main.x, align 8
 i32Stored value type does not match pointer operand type!
  store i8* getelementptr inbounds ([6 x i8], [6 x i8]* @1, i32 0, i32 0), i32* @main.x, align 8
 i32
code generation error: asr_to_llvm: module failed verification. Error:
Stored value type does not match pointer operand type!
  store i8* getelementptr inbounds ([6 x i8], [6 x i8]* @0, i32 0, i32 0), i32* @main.x, align 8
 i32Stored value type does not match pointer operand type!
  store i8* getelementptr inbounds ([6 x i8], [6 x i8]* @1, i32 0, i32 0), i32* @main.x, align 8
 i32


Note: Please report unclear, confusing or incorrect messages as bugs at
https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/issues.
```
When I compile the same program using gfortran, I get the expected semantic error:
``` bash 
gfortran test.f90
test.f90:2:18:

    2 |     integer :: x = 'Apple'
      |                  1
Error: Cannot convert CHARACTER(5) to INTEGER(4) at (1)
```
LFortran should throw a ``semantic error`` instead of proceeding to code generation and ultimately resulting in a ``code generation error.``  The error message should indicate that the string 'Apple' cannot be assigned to an integer. 
I would also like to help resolve this Negative indices or indices below 1, like s(-1:4), are invalid and should throw an error at compile-time.
LFortran does not provide the same check and simply executes the code, gives incorrect behavior and to fix that add a check in the lfortran compiler that detects this invalid index at compile time and throws an appropriate error
So i need to find where string section handling (substring extraction) is implemented in LFortran this is in the ast_body_visitor.cpp ?

Minimal Reproducible Example

```fortran
PROGRAM main
   IMPLICIT NONE
   INTEGER :: i
   INTEGER :: j

   i = 1
   j = 0

   PRINT *, i/j

END PROGRAM main
```
### Output
#### LFortran
```console
(base) saurabh-kumar@Awadh:~/Projects/System/lfortran$ lfortran ./examples/example.f90
-1
```
It is strange that GFortran does not catch this error either and fails with a `Floating point exception (core dumped)`.
